next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

They had developed a routine for the examinations, and the routine itself was a kind of intimacy neither of them acknowledged.

She would set out her diagnostic instruments on the library's long table — the resonance crystals, the notation ledger, the stabilization framework she'd designed specifically for the Mark's magical signature. He would roll up his left sleeve and lay his forearm on the padded surface they'd improvised from a folded cloak, and then he would pick up whatever he was reading with his free hand, as if the examination were no more notable than having his pulse taken at a routine physical.

It hadn't always been like this. The first examinations had been rigid, formal — his jaw set, her instructions precise, both of them maintaining the clinical frame with an effort that was itself a kind of confession. Now he read during examinations. She hummed sometimes, without meaning to, when the diagnostic results were interesting. He'd told her once that the humming was how he knew she'd found something, and she'd been self-conscious about it for two days before deciding that self-consciousness about humming was its own pointless category of intimacy she didn't need.

This morning the library was doing its usual thing — fire lit, lamps adjusted to the grey December light, the room's configuration subtly rearranged since yesterday so that the working table sat closer to the hearth and the windows showed a slightly different angle of the grounds. Frost on the lawns. The last leaves gone from the elms. She'd stopped trying to catalogue the Manor's daily...
open_hooks_to_carry: Post-drawing-room gentleness rendered in small domestic moments: shared meals, books left on chairs, tea made without asking
Draco cataloguing Hermione's expressions — her laugh, her sleeping in the library — registering that he is doing this
Theo Nott's visit: Draco with a friend, drier and funnier, the dynamic revealing who he is when not armored
prev_tail_excerpt:
...r the glamour. She could feel each letter, a hot pulse that followed the sequence — M-U-D-B-L-O-O-D — as if the wound were reciting itself. She pressed her thumb against the inside of her wrist, hard, over the start of the M, and held it there until the throbbing compressed into a single point of pressure, sharp and specific, and then she let go and the throbbing spread back along the letters like water filling a channel.

The teacup from earlier was on her bedside table. She'd carried it up from the library two nights ago and hadn't brought it back down. The milk residue had dried to a skin on the inside. He'd made her that cup of tea. He'd known the milk. He'd sat across from her in the warm library and winced when her neck popped and made a dry joke about straight lines and cooked her overcooked lamb and none of it, none of it, cancelled the drawing room. She wanted it to. She wanted the ordinary hours to weigh more than the worst one. She wanted the arithmetic to come out differently and it didn't, and wanting it to was not the same as it being so, and she was so tired of knowing the difference.

The Manor was quiet around her. No shifting corridors, no adjusting temperature, no surfaces tending to her needs. The house was still, and whether the stillness was guilt or indifference or the simple mechanical absence of the conditions that made it move, she couldn't tell.

Through the resonance, faint and bruised, she felt Malfoy two floors below. Still in the drawing room. Still on the floor.

She pulled the blanket over her shoulders and sat in the dark and did not sleep.
secondary_character_beats: Theo Nott as a full person: post-war Slytherin navigating suspicion, pragmatic, bitter in flashes, genuinely loyal. He sees the situation with Hermione immediately and says almost nothing about it except one honest line to Draco alone
state_deltas_required: The aftermath of trauma confrontation — both characters navigating a new register of honesty while trying to continue the work
Tenderness enters without permission. Domesticity becomes habitual. A third register emerges between them: not professional, not traumatic, but quiet and almost fond
